text: {"claims":[{"statement":"Potassium, calcium, iron, iodine, folate, vitamin D and fibre are highlighted by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) as nutrients of concern for the European population.","claimType":"definitional","quote":"Potassium, calcium, iron, iodine, folate, vitamin D and fibre are highlighted by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) as nutrients of concern for the European population","entailment":"The quoted sentence directly states that EFSA highlights those nutrients as concerns for the European population.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"definitional"},{"statement":"Choline, vitamin D, folate and iodine are provided by eggs in clinically useful quantities.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Of these, choline, vitamin D, folate and iodine are provided by eggs in clinically useful quantities.","entailment":"The sentence reports that eggs contain clinically useful amounts of those nutrients, which is an empirical observation.","population":"","intervention":"eggs","comparator":"","outcome":"providing choline, vitamin D, folate and iodine","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"clinically useful quantities","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Choline is an essential micronutrient for infant growth and development and is typically found in eggs and beef liver, with lower amounts in plant-based foods such as almonds and broccoli.","claimType":"definitional","quote":"Choline is an essential micronutrient for infant growth and development and is typically found in eggs and beef liver, with lower amounts in plant-based foods such as almonds and broccoli","entailment":"The quoted text defines choline’s role and typical food sources, providing a factual definition.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"definitional"},{"statement":"The Average Intake (AI) set by the EFSA are 400 mg/day for adolescents and adults; and 480 mg/day during pregnancy.","claimType":"definitional","quote":"Recommendations have been set by some expert bodies; for example, the Average Intake (AI) set by the EFSA are 400 mg/day for adolescents and adults; and 480 mg/day during pregnancy.","entailment":"The sentence states the EFSA AI values, which is a definitional claim about recommended intake levels.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"400 mg/day for adolescents and adults; 480 mg/day during pregnancy","modality":"definitional"},{"statement":"The values set by US health bodies are 425 mg for female adults; 550 mg for male adults; and 450 mg during pregnancy.","claimType":"definitional","quote":"The values set by US health bodies are 425 mg for female adults; 550 mg for male adults; and 450 mg during pregnancy.","entailment":"The quoted sentence provides the US recommended choline intake values, a definitional statement.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"425 mg for female adults; 550 mg for male adults; 450 mg during pregnancy","modality":"definitional"},{"statement":"An analysis of choline intake in nine European countries found that the average intake was well below that of the American AI.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"An analysis of choline intake in nine European countries found that the average intake was well below that of the American AI","entailment":"The sentence reports the result of an analysis, indicating lower intake than the American AI, which is an empirical finding.","population":"people in nine European countries","intervention":"","comparator":"American AI","outcome":"average choline intake","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"well below","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Only 8.5% of women met the American AI for choline according to a nationwide survey.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Choline intakes are also low in pregnancy, with only 8.5% of women meeting the American AI according to a nationwide survey","entailment":"The quoted text provides a specific proportion of women meeting the AI, an empirical observation.","population":"pregnant women","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"meeting the American AI for choline","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"only 8.5%","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Increasing choline intake during pregnancy above the AI may benefit infant cognitive development.","claimType":"predictive","quote":"There is some evidence that increasing choline intake during pregnancy above the AI may benefit infant cognitive development","entailment":"The sentence suggests a possible benefit, indicating a predictive/conditional claim about infant cognition.","population":"pregnant women and their infants","intervention":"increasing choline intake above the AI","comparator":"AI level","outcome":"infant cognitive development","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"conditional"},{"statement":"Daily choline supplementation (930 mg, roughly twice the AI) in the third trimester of pregnancy improved infant information processing speed at 4–13 months compared with supplementing at the current AI of 480 mg/day.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Two studies found that daily choline supplementation (930 mg, roughly twice the AI) in the third trimester of pregnancy improved infant information processing speed at 4–13 months and improved sustained attention at 7 years compared with supplementing at the current AI of 480 mg/day","entailment":"The quoted sentence reports study results showing improved infant outcomes with higher choline supplementation, an empirical claim.","population":"infants whose mothers took choline supplementation in third trimester","intervention":"daily choline supplementation 930 mg","comparator":"supplementation at AI 480 mg/day","outcome":"information processing speed at 4–13 months and sustained attention at 7 years","timeframe":"4–13 months and 7 years","quantifiers":"930 mg (roughly twice the AI)","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"Egg consumers had significantly greater choline intakes compared with non-consumers (525 mg/day vs. 294 mg/day, p < 0.0001).","claimType":"empirical","quote":"The previously mentioned study by Wallace et al. found that egg consumers had significantly greater choline intakes compared with non-consumers (525 mg/day vs. 294 mg/day, p < 0.0001)","entailment":"The quoted result directly states a statistical difference in choline intake between egg consumers and non‑consumers, an empirical finding.","population":"egg consumers and non‑consumers","intervention":"egg consumption","comparator":"non‑consumption","outcome":"choline intake","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"525 mg/day vs. 294 mg/day, p < 0.0001","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"Participants given daily whole egg, but not a yolk-free egg substitute, had significantly higher plasma choline levels compared with baseline (p = 0.01).","claimType":"empirical","quote":"The efficacy of whole eggs for raising plasma choline levels is confirmed by another RCT where participants given daily whole egg, but not a yolk-free egg substitute, had significantly higher plasma choline levels compared with baseline ( p = 0.01)","entailment":"The quoted RCT result indicates that whole‑egg consumption raised plasma choline levels, an empirical claim.","population":"participants in the RCT","intervention":"daily whole egg","comparator":"yolk‑free egg substitute / baseline","outcome":"plasma choline levels","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"p = 0.01","modality":"causal"}]}
